notes.completion_rate: Perfect 100% completion. Strong execution despite high rollover volume.
notes.oos_total: Zero scope creep. Disciplined sprint planning maintained throughout.
notes.rollover: 23 items carried forward (62% of sprint). High rollover indicates dependency chains or underestimation in prior sprints.
notes.cycle_time: 9d median exceeds healthy range. Review blockers and handoff delays slowing item progression.
notes.type_split: 30% bugs is healthy. Story-driven sprint with balanced maintenance work.

notes.completion_rate: Excellent 96% completion demonstrates strong execution and accurate sprint planning.
notes.oos_total: Zero scope changes reflects disciplined planning and effective backlog prioritization.
notes.rollover: 31% rollover (8 items) is concerning—indicates underestimation or blockers from prior sprint. Review sizing accuracy.
notes.cycle_time: 10d median cycle time exceeds healthy range (target <7d). Investigate workflow bottlenecks and WIP limits.
notes.type_split: 50% bugs is elevated—signals technical debt or quality issues upstream. Consider root cause analysis and prevention strategies.

notes.completion_rate: 70% completion is below healthy threshold. 7 items incomplete signals scope or capacity issues that need review.
notes.oos_total: Zero out-of-sprint additions shows disciplined planning and minimal reactive work.
notes.rollover: 5 items rolled over (22% of sprint) — investigate blockers or estimation accuracy.
notes.cycle_time: 7d median cycle time is at upper boundary of normal range. Flag for process friction or review bottlenecks.
notes.type_split: 48% bugs is high and indicates quality debt. Prioritize root cause analysis and preventive measures.

notes.completion_rate: 14% completion is critically low for a closed sprint. Only 3 of 21 items delivered suggests significant scope misjudgment or blocking issues.
notes.oos_total: 1 OOS item indicates disciplined sprint planning despite poor execution.
notes.rollover: 8 items rolled over (38%) signals persistent capacity mismatch. Investigate recurring blockers and refine velocity estimates.
notes.cycle_time: 33d median cycle time is severely elevated. Items are stalling in progress—identify bottlenecks immediately.
notes.type_split: 10% bugs is healthy, with 62% stories indicating feature-focused work. Type balance not the issue here.

notes.completion_rate: 86% complete mid-sprint shows strong execution velocity. On track for healthy close if current pace holds.
notes.oos_total: Only 1 OOS item reflects excellent sprint discipline and stable planning.
notes.rollover: 19 items rolled over (66%) — extremely high carryover signals either chronic overcommitment or blockers preventing closure in prior sprint. Investigate root cause.
notes.cycle_time: 26d median cycle time is severely elevated and indicates major blockers, complex work, or items stalling in-progress. Needs immediate attention.
notes.type_split: 24% bugs is healthy. Story-driven sprint with balanced maintenance load.

notes.completion_rate: Only 11% complete mid-sprint with 3 items carried over from prior sprint—trajectory suggests significant carryover risk into Sprint 33.
notes.oos_total: 1 item added after sprint start—planning discipline holding.
notes.rollover: 3 items rolled over (33% of sprint)—investigate blockers and sizing accuracy.
notes.cycle_time: 22d median cycle time is severely elevated—items stuck in progress, needs immediate review.
notes.type_split: 56% bugs signals reactive work dominating sprint—root cause analysis needed to reduce defect rate.

notes.total: 101 tickets scoped across 6 sprints is a healthy pipeline for the quarter. With 77% of the quarter elapsed and 92% completion rate, the team is on track to close the quarter strong.
notes.completed: 93 of 101 tickets completed with 21 days remaining positions the team well to finish the remaining 7 in-progress items before quarter end. Velocity of 1.33 tickets/day is consistent and sustainable.
notes.completion_rate: 92% completion rate with 76% of quarter elapsed indicates strong on-track delivery; 8 tickets remain in progress or open, manageable for final sprint.
notes.releases_shipped: 3 releases shipped this quarter with the most recent on May 28th aligns with healthy mobile app cadence. Final release window is available before June 30th if needed for remaining work.
notes.oos_total: Only 1 out-of-sprint ticket (1% of total) reflects disciplined sprint planning. Excellent hygiene.
notes.oos_open: Zero open out-of-sprint items — all OOS work has been resolved. Only 1 OOS ticket all quarter (1%) demonstrates disciplined sprint planning and minimal reactive interrupt work.
notes.type_split: Bug ratio at 42% (42 of 101 tickets) is elevated and signals quality debt requiring attention. Sprints 28 and 29 ran at 50% and 48% bugs respectively; Sprint 32 is currently 56% bugs, suggesting the pattern is worsening rather than improving.
notes.avg_releases: Median of 1 release per sprint is appropriate for mobile app deployment cycles constrained by app store review. 3 releases across 5 sprints demonstrates consistent delivery rhythm.
notes.time_logged: 574 hours logged against 1,272 hours estimated represents 45% utilisation, which is concerningly low. Giuseppe Maffia logged only 123h against 530h estimated; Gianni Toniolo logged 8h against 90h estimated. Either time tracking discipline has collapsed or estimates are systematically inflated by 2-3x — both scenarios undermine planning reliability.
notes.estimate_accuracy: Team consistently logs ~45% of estimated hours with -55% variance — estimates are systematically too high, suggesting poor calibration or tracking gaps rather than scope creep.
notes.no_estimate: 26% of tickets (26 of 101) have no time estimate, making capacity planning unreliable. Combined with the 55% estimate variance and severe under-logging, the team's estimation and tracking hygiene needs immediate attention to restore forecasting accuracy.
notes.rollover: 3 rollovers (3%) is minimal and healthy overall. However, Sprint 31 carried a 66% rollover rate (19 of 29 tickets), which compressed the sprint and inflated completion metrics — this warrants review of sprint 31 planning assumptions.
notes.cycle_time: Median cycle time of 10 days is reasonable for mobile development. Mean of 15.3 days indicates some longer-running tickets are skewing the average; recent sprints show increasing cycle times (Sprint 31: 26 days median) worth monitoring.
notes.assignee_workload: Giuseppe Maffia carries 53% of all quarter tickets (54 of 101), which is disproportionately high and a burnout risk; Fabrizio De Marco (26 tickets, 26%) and other team members are handling significantly lighter loads, suggesting workload rebalancing is needed.
